index.metaDescription: Kevin Lee — Software Engineer, Pilot, Photographer. Building at Paramount.
index.bio1: I'm Kevin — a senior software engineer at Paramount working on video gisting and short-form content with applied ML. Before that I was at Microsoft AI on Copilot, interned at NASA JPL, studied CS at USC, and served in the Army.
index.bio2: On the side I run a small software company called PookieB — 10 products and counting across web, iOS, and AI/ML. I like building things that are genuinely useful.
index.bio3: When I'm not coding, I'm usually flying. I volunteer with Angel Flight West doing medical transport flights around the Pacific Northwest. I also surf, take photos, and travel whenever I can.
index.dispatchSubtitle: A weekly letter to friends and family.

resume.metaDescription: Resume — Kevin Lee, Software Engineering Manager, Applied AI/ML at Paramount.
resume.heading: Resume
resume.intro: I build at the intersection of scale and intelligence. Today I lead the Applied ML team for Short Form at Paramount, architecting production AI pipelines — multimodal embeddings, semantic retrieval, classification — across one of the world's largest media catalogs. Before that I shipped Microsoft Copilot to millions, mapped brain regions at the Broad Institute, optimized the Curiosity rover at NASA JPL, and served five years in the U.S. Army. On weekends I fly medical transport for Angel Flight West.
resume.paramountTitle: Paramount — Software Engineering Manager, Applied AI/ML
resume.paramountDesc: Lead the Applied ML team for Short Form, building end-to-end AI workflows for content classification, multimodal embedding, semantic retrieval, and personalization. Architected a Google Cloud pipeline that turns raw media into searchable intelligence — Gemini multimodal embeddings, LLM taxonomy classification, output validation — powering a clip-classification platform (FastAPI · Cloud SQL · Pub/Sub · GKE) at ~2s p50 and ~$0.0003 per clip, replacing manual editorial review at catalog scale.
resume.msftDesc: Shipped the new Microsoft Copilot settings experience end-to-end on Azure, serving millions of daily users. Designed and operated Cosmos data pipelines ingesting 40TB+/day across Copilot, Edge, and Windows (100M+ monthly users), cutting experimentation latency from 72 to 24 hours; migrating always-on VMs to event-driven Azure Functions trimmed $240K+/yr in compute at 99.9%+ uptime. Led a 12-person team to win the 2022 Microsoft Global Hackathon with an AI tool for early Alzheimer's detection.
resume.broadTitle: Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard — Researcher
resume.broadDesc: In Dr. Guoping Feng's lab, classified spatial scRNA-seq (MERFISH) to subthalamic nuclei regions using machine learning on DAPI/poly-T scans from the Allen Brain Institute.
resume.armyDesc: Five years of service. Led and trained squads of 8–12 through tactical field exercises, then ran logistics as Battalion S-4 for a 500+ person battalion. Full Army ROTC scholarship.
resume.nasaTitle: NASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory — Software Engineering Intern
resume.nasaDesc: Built scheduling-optimization models for Curiosity rover operations, improving mission-planning efficiency 8–12% and cutting idle time between science activities. Presented the work at the 2020 NASA conference.
resume.selectedWorkIntro: Side projects shipped under my studio, PookieB — across web, iOS, and AI/ML.
resume.swShredders: Shredders — real-time powder tracker, 53 resorts, 90 API routes, built on Next.js.
resume.swSpacec: SPACEc — Stanford / Nolan-Lab Python workflow for multiplexed-imaging analysis, published in Nature Communications; on PyPI.
resume.swSmartSpender: SmartSpender — AI financial-decision engine, a 3-stage pipeline across 13 AWS Lambdas.
resume.uscDegree: University of Southern California — B.S. Computer Science, Viterbi School of Engineering · 2022
resume.uscHonors: Honors — Army ROTC & Pathway Oregon full scholarships · Armed Forces Award · Dean's List
resume.afwDesc: Volunteer pilot flying medical transport across the Puget Sound for Angel Flight West.
resume.skillsLanguages: Languages: Python, C++, C#, TypeScript, Java, SCOPE
resume.skillsAI: AI / ML: Gemini, multimodal embeddings, LLM classification, PyTorch, TensorFlow
resume.skillsSystems: Cloud & Infra: GCP (Vertex AI, GKE, Cloud SQL, Pub/Sub), Azure (Functions, Cosmos), AWS (Lambda, DynamoDB), Kubernetes, Docker
resume.interestsList: Humanitarian aid · sustainable energy · surfing · flying · traveling
resume.aiCta: Want to ask about any of this? Ask my AI — it answers as me.
resume.chatSubtitle: Curious about my experience? Ask below.
resume.chatDisclaimer: Powered by AI · Responses based on Kevin's resume
resume.connectSubtitle: Want to connect? Book a time that works for you.
resume.chatErrorRateLimit: You're sending messages too fast. Please wait a minute before trying again.

resume.sn1: MERFISH images individual RNA molecules right where they sit in tissue. I later rebuilt the whole pipeline as a case study.
resume.sn2: A Battalion S-4 runs supply, maintenance, and movement for a 500-soldier unit — logistics at a scale that turned out to be good practice for distributed systems.

projects.metaDescription: Projects by Kevin Lee — software engineer building across web, iOS, and AI/ML.
projects.heading: Projects
projects.subtitle: Things I've built across web, iOS, and AI/ML. Most of these live under PookieB.
projects.featuredShreddersDesc: Real-time powder conditions for 53 ski resorts. Pulls snowpack, forecasts, road conditions, and webcams into a single score, with trip planning to sort out who's driving. Web and iOS.
projects.featuredSpacecDesc: A Stanford Python library for multiplexed imaging analysis, covering the full pipeline from cell segmentation to spatial analysis. Introduces Patch Proximity Analysis. Published on PyPI and bioRxiv.
projects.shreddersDesc: Real-time snow conditions and powder scoring for PNW ski resorts.
projects.newsDesc: Pulls headlines from nine sources, summarizes them, and turns the day's news into a short podcast in a custom ElevenLabs voice. iOS app with widgets.
projects.smartspenderDesc: Ask a money question in plain English, get back real math and an explanation. Runs the numbers deterministically rather than letting the model guess, reads receipts with GPT-4o Vision, and has a SwiftUI app for splitting expenses. Built on AWS Lambda and Bedrock.
projects.daybydayDesc: A journaling app built around showing up. Photos, streaks, an accountability partner, and trip planning on MapKit. iOS.
projects.harmonyDesc: An iOS app for couples: pair with your partner, build shared habit trackers, and get coaching prompts on how you communicate. WidgetKit on the home screen.
projects.wilcoDesc: Radio practice for student pilots learning to talk to air traffic control. You speak the call, it scores your phraseology against ICAO standards, and the scenarios run on live METAR/TAF weather.
projects.coletteDesc: A language tutor that holds a conversation in five languages, drills vocabulary with spaced repetition, and remembers the mistakes you keep making so it can come back to them. Next.js and Supabase.
projects.spacecDesc: Stanford library for multiplexed imaging and spatial analysis.
projects.aduDesc: Checks whether a San Jose lot can add a backyard unit. Runs the city's permit eligibility rules against government GIS data: flood zones, fire risk, zoning, and building footprints.
projects.merfishDesc: A spatial-transcriptomics pipeline for Vizgen MERSCOPE mouse brain. QC against bulk RNAseq, Scanpy clustering, cell-type mapping with a per-cell confidence score, and registration of each section to the Allen CCFv3 atlas with STalign. Covered by an offline pytest suite and CI.

brock.pageTitle: Brock — A Year in the Life
brock.metaDescription: Brock — A photo story by Kevin Lee
brock.heroSubtitle: A year in the life of
brock.heroDesc: From city sidewalks to coastal cliffs, ER waiting rooms to autumn leaves. One little golden doodle, one very big year.
brock.ch1Tagline: Life in San Francisco. Coffee shops, coding sessions, and couch naps.
brock.ch1Caption1: The face of a dog who has fully claimed the couch.
brock.ch1Text1: Every morning, the same routine. Kevin codes. Brock supervises. The best coworker never sends a Slack message — he just nudges your hand when it's time for a walk.
brock.ch1Text2: Post-bath blowout. Not a fan of the hair dryer — but absolutely a fan of being fluffy.
brock.ch2Tagline: The late-night drives. The waiting room. The relief.
brock.ch2Caption1: Wrapped up safe. Everything's going to be okay.
brock.ch2Text1: Sterile panniculitis. Words no puppy parent wants to hear. It meant multiple ER visits, sleepless nights, and a kind of fear that made everything else disappear. It freaked Mom and Dad out — a lot. But Dr. Schnabl was a savior, and Brock pulled through. He's been blessed ever since.
brock.ch3Tagline: Coastlines, canoes, wildflowers, and the best camping buddy you could ask for.
brock.ch3Caption1: First mate Brock. Yellow vest, tongue out, ready for anything.
brock.ch3Text1: When his little legs got tired on the trail, Beryl carried him like a baby. He didn't seem to mind one bit.
brock.ch3Text2: Camping with a mini goldendoodle means sharing your sleeping bag, finding fur in your coffee, and waking up to a face that says: can we do this forever?
brock.ch4Tagline: San Francisco → South Bend, Indiana. 2,200 miles. One very good co-pilot.
brock.ch4Text1: Airplane carriers, golden hour on the highway, and that first night in a half-empty apartment surrounded by boxes. Home isn't the city. Home is whoever's on the couch with you.
brock.ch5Tagline: South Bend, Indiana. New town, same good boy.
brock.ch5Caption1: First fall in Indiana. The leaves didn't disappoint.
brock.ch5Text1: Notre Dame bandana on, blessed in the spirit of St. Francis. Technically, the first Catholic in the family. He moved Kevin and Beryl so much that day, they both joined OCIA. Leave it to a 15-pound goldendoodle to lead his parents to faith.
brock.ch5Text2: Dinner parties in the new kitchen. Movie nights with his favorite Mr. Bunny. Some things never change — and that's the whole point.
brock.epilogueText: Cities change. Zip codes change. But the little guy on the couch? He just wants to be wherever you are. And honestly, that's enough.
brock.footer: Made with love by Kevin & Beryl — klee.page

merfish.metaDescription: Case study: a spatial-transcriptomics pipeline that turns Vizgen MERSCOPE mouse-brain data into cell-type-mapped, atlas-registered maps — one RNA molecule at a time.
merfish.exploreHint: move to stir · drag to pan · scroll for story
merfish.heroSub: Read the RNA inside an intact mouse brain — one molecule at a time — then rebuild the brain from where those molecules sit.
merfish.lede: MERFISH (multiplexed error-robust fluorescence in situ hybridization) images individual RNA molecules right where they sit in tissue. Hundreds of genes per cell, positions kept — so the anatomy comes from the molecules themselves.
merfish.introNote: Portfolio repo, not a production pipeline: notebooks and pytest walk a Vizgen coronal cohort through QC, clustering, cell-type mapping, and CCF registration. Synthetic fixtures run offline in CI; live tests hit public downloads when you have network.
merfish.methodKicker: Reading a gene as a barcode
merfish.methodTitle: Every gene gets an error-correcting binary code.
merfish.methodP1: MERFISH reads each gene as a binary codeword spread across many imaging rounds — the on/off pattern names the gene. The code self-corrects (16-bit, Hamming-distance-4), so one misread round can't change the answer. That's the "ER" in MERFISH.
merfish.methodNote: Illustrative codewords (weight-4, Hamming distance 4). A real panel spreads hundreds of genes across the rounds, and misreading one round can't flip a call to another valid gene.
merfish.pipelineP: Segmentation comes first — it decides what counts as a cell — then QC, clustering, mapping, and atlas registration hand off through the same object.
merfish.validationP: MERFISH counts should match bulk RNAseq and reproduce across replicates in the MsBrain_VS38 aging cohort — rendered with seaborn from cohort data.
merfish.stat1Label: Pearson r · MERFISH vs bulk RNAseq (per gene)
merfish.qcCaption: MERSCOPE QC summary. Yield, replicate reproducibility, and the MERFISH↔bulk-RNAseq check, rendered with seaborn from cohort data.
merfish.celltypesTitle: Cell types with a confidence score.
merfish.celltypesP: I reimplemented the Allen cell_type_mapper approach on Moffitt 2018 hypothalamus hold-out data: marker-gene matching plus a bootstrap confidence that beats the old cosine heuristic (label only, no score). Fine subtype accuracy within correct classes: 0.82.
merfish.celltypesCaption: Cell-type mapping with a confidence score. Accuracy rises with confidence (top-left); correct calls concentrate at high confidence (top-right); a coronal slice (bottom-right) flags where the 161-gene panel can't resolve a type. The cosine heuristic gives none of this.
merfish.ependymalCaption: Cluster 22 — ependymal / glial. Aqp4, Gfap, Mlc1 lining the ventricles.
merfish.inhibitoryCaption: Cluster 24 — inhibitory neurons. Gad1, Slc32a1, Cckar, recovered bilaterally.
merfish.segmentationP: Draw the cell boundary wrong and everything downstream shifts. On shared ground truth, transcript-aware assignment hits 0.76 accuracy (Voronoi 0.70, nucleus-only 0.17); downstream Leiden ARI reaches 1.00 vs 0.96 baseline. Three methods benchmarked end-to-end (Baysor / proseg / segger-style).
merfish.segmentationCaption: Segmentation changes downstream cell types. Transcript-aware assignment (Baysor / proseg / segger-style) rescues the boundary molecules a Voronoi baseline misassigns. A guarded cellpose_sam_segment() hook runs Cellpose-SAM on DAPI mosaics when installed.
merfish.registrationKicker: The hardest part of a new scan
merfish.registrationP: Each cell needs a brain-region label — anchor the AP plane, warp with STalign, lift labels from the Allen atlas. The registration core adds per-cell confidence and a QC score that separates good fits from deliberately bad ones (0.14→0.96 on synthetic misalignment). High-confidence calls hit ~98–99% at every ontology depth on that synthetic geometry; 670 leaf regions roll up the ontology tree.
merfish.stalignP: On a slice cut from a known plane, STalign places it to 46.7 µm — under half a voxel — and gets 0.89 of per-cell labels right, in about two minutes on CPU. A real 2D→3D fit, not a stub.
merfish.stalignCaption: STalign 2D→3D, validated. Input section (left), regions STalign assigns after warping into the CCFv3 (center, near-identical), and the recovered AP position peaking at the true plane (right).
merfish.anchorP: STalign can misfire on its starting guess — a posterior slice landed 1.44 mm off before the anchor. Sliding the section against every atlas plane fixes depth first: posterior AP error drops 1440→148 µm, thalamus IoU on that scan goes 0.23→0.68, and all three thalamic test sections align (was 1/3). Anchor logic is unit-tested; posterior recovery is live-tested.
merfish.thalamusCaption: Thalamus across three scans. Truth (crimson, top) vs recovered by STalign→CCFv3 (green, bottom), including the posterior bilateral clusters the bare fit missed. The anchor is unit-tested; the posterior recovery is live-tested.
merfish.proofP: Moffitt et al. hypothalamic MERFISH — 73,626 cells, 160 genes. Unsupervised Leiden (27 clusters) vs 16 published classes: ARI 0.28 (random ≈ 0), concordant UMAP, real coronal anatomy.
merfish.proofCaption: Live validation on real MERFISH. Unsupervised Leiden (27 clusters) vs 16 published classes: concordant UMAP, coronal anatomy, and a near-diagonal concordance heatmap. Generated by the pytest suite (34 offline / 41 total, 7 live); offline tests run in CI on Python 3.12.
merfish.contextKicker: Where MERFISH sits in the field
merfish.contextP: MERFISH is one of three imaging platforms that read single molecules in place. Imaging trades panel size for subcellular precision; sequencing trades precision for whole-transcriptome reach.
merfish.contextNote: The field is standardizing on SpatialData, segmentation is the active research front, and panels keep growing toward whole-transcriptome imaging.
merfish.scopeP: Light core in CI; heavy atlases and engines plug in behind the same interface.
merfish.scopeImplemented: Scanpy pipeline, cell-type mapper, segmentation benchmark, and the registration core (geometry, label transfer, uncertainty, QC), all covered by offline pytest.
merfish.scopeValidated: Allen CCFv3 via brainglobe, and the STalign LDDMM deformable backend with a ground-truthed fit, in an isolated NumPy-2 / Python-3.12 environment.
merfish.scopeStubbed: DeepSlice, ANTs, and the Allen cell_type_mapper are stubbed behind the live interfaces with install hints. They need large downloads, not new architecture.
merfish.scopeCaveat: Honest caveat (also in the repo): the calibration and QC numbers use a synthetic registration error on real atlas geometry — so they test the math, not an engine's end-to-end accuracy. The STalign result is a real fit.
merfish.notesP: Cell-type and region calls both ship calibrated confidence — same pattern, two stages. Every stage has a no-download synthetic path so pytest runs in CI without atlas downloads; real engines slot in behind the same function signatures. STalign upstream still pins numpy==1.23; I install it --no-deps beside NumPy 2.4 in one venv rather than maintaining a separate STalign-only environment.
merfish.footer: © {year} Kevin Lee · built with scanpy, STalign & the Allen CCFv3

shredders.metaDescription: Case study: Shredders — real-time powder conditions for 53 ski resorts across 11 regions.
shredders.tagline: Real-time powder conditions for 53 ski resorts across 11 regions.
shredders.stats: 53 resorts · 11 regions · web + iOS · seven data sources
shredders.galleryCaption: iOS app — Today's forecast and Mountain rankings
shredders.problemP1: Planning a ski day means checking five different websites. SNOTEL for snowpack. NOAA for the forecast. WSDOT for road conditions. The resort's webcam page. Your group chat for who's actually going. By the time you've pieced it together, the alarm is set for 5 AM and you've talked yourself out of it.
shredders.problemP2: Shredders pulls all of that into one place, on web and iOS: a powder score, 7-day forecasts, weather alerts, an AI chat for trip planning, and the social bits for sorting out who's driving.
shredders.architectureP1: The backend is a Next.js app. Routes are grouped by what they serve: mountains (conditions, forecasts, powder score, alerts, webcams, lifts, parking, trip advice), auth (email and Apple Sign-In), events (RSVP, carpool, recurring series), social (check-ins, likes, photos), and AI chat. Three Vercel cron jobs send daily event reminders, weather alerts, and powder notifications.
shredders.architectureP2: Storage is Supabase Postgres. The schema covers users, events and everything attached to them (attendees, comments, photos, activity feeds, date polls, invite tokens), social features, push tokens, alert subscriptions, sessions, audit logs, mountain status, and scraper health.
shredders.powderScoreP: One number from 0 to 100 that answers a single question: is it worth the drive? It weighs recent snowfall (24h, 48h, 7d), temperature at elevation, wind, visibility, current conditions, snow still in the forecast, base depth, and seasonal trends.
shredders.performanceP: The first version of the frontend fired off dozens of API calls on page load. Batch endpoints brought that down to three. A 10-minute response cache keeps Supabase query volume in check, and the iOS app launches with two calls. Images are AVIF/WebP and lazy-loaded.
shredders.whySupabaseP: I wanted real-time subscriptions, auth, and a relational database in one place, and I didn't want to run infrastructure for a side project. Supabase gave me Postgres with real SQL, built-in auth that handles Apple Sign-In, and auto-generated APIs. The auto-generated APIs are quick to build against, but you give up fine control over query tuning as the number of endpoints grows.
shredders.whyScrapeP: No resort offers a usable API. Lift status, webcam URLs, parking capacity all live on PHP pages that haven't been touched since 2014. Cheerio parses the static HTML, Puppeteer handles the JavaScript-rendered pages. It's fragile: every time Crystal Mountain redesigns their snow report, a scraper breaks. So I track scraper health in its own tables and check it on a cron.
shredders.whyDualHeading: Why dual platform from day one?
shredders.whyDualP: People check conditions at 5 AM from bed, deciding whether to make the drive. That's a phone moment. The web app is better for planning, with the bigger screen, map view, and event coordination, but the iOS app is where most of the use happens. SwiftUI was quick to build, and powder-alert push notifications only make sense on the phone.
shredders.whyRoutesHeading: Why so many small API routes?
shredders.whyRoutesP: Each route does one thing, so each one is easy to reason about. Lots of small endpoints over a few big ones let me move fast: add a feature, add a route. The cost is surface area. A refactor touches a lot of files, and the frontend got chatty, which is what the batch endpoints fixed.
shredders.tradeoffsScraping: Scraping is fragile. Resort sites change without warning. Monitoring catches the failures quickly, but a broken scraper still means missing data until I fix the parser. There's no way around that without official APIs.
shredders.tradeoffsFreeTiers: Free tiers shape the architecture. Supabase and Vercel free tiers are generous but not infinite. Upstash Redis keeps Supabase query volume down, and batch endpoints keep Vercel invocations reasonable. At ten times the usage I'd need paid tiers or a different host.
shredders.tradeoffsDualAuth: Dual auth is a lot for a side project. Supporting both email/password and Apple Sign-In means JWT rotation, token blacklisting, session tracking, and rate limiting across two flows. An iOS app needs it, but it's more auth code than a side project usually warrants.
shredders.sn1: SNOTEL — SNOwpack TELemetry — is the USDA’s network of automated snow sensors across the western mountains.
shredders.sn2: Dozens of calls on page load wasn’t a correctness bug. It was a melts-on-a-phone-in-the-parking-lot-with-one-bar bug.

spacec.metaDescription: Case study: SPACEc — Stanford academic Python library for multiplexed imaging analysis.
spacec.tagline: A Stanford library for multiplexed imaging analysis, from cell segmentation through spatial analysis.
spacec.stats: Python · six core modules · published on PyPI and bioRxiv
spacec.logoIntro: An interactive Python workflow for multiplexed image processing, from tissue extraction to spatial analysis, in one library.
spacec.problemP1: Multiplexed imaging lets researchers see 40+ proteins in a single tissue section, which matters for studying how cells interact in cancer, autoimmunity, and neuroscience. But the analysis is stitched together from separate tools: Cellpose for segmentation, scanpy for clustering, custom scripts for spatial analysis, matplotlib for visualization.
spacec.problemP2: That means more time spent wrangling code than doing science. Each lab ends up with its own collection of notebooks that break when a dependency updates.
spacec.ideaP: One library that handles the whole workflow, from raw TIFF images to spatial results, behind a consistent API. SPACEc follows the scanpy convention: sp.tl (tools), sp.pp (preprocessing), sp.pl (plotting), sp.hf (helpers). It's built on AnnData, the standard format in single-cell biology.
spacec.overviewCaption: Full pipeline overview from the SPACEc paper
spacec.patchNote: *Patch Proximity Analysis is the novel research contribution.
spacec.module1: sp.tl._general — core analysis, clustering, annotation, spatial
spacec.patchP1: Standard neighborhood analysis works on cell-to-cell distances. It tells you cell A is near cell B, but not how groups of cells are organized at the tissue level.
spacec.patchP2: Patch Proximity Analysis takes a different route. DBSCAN finds spatial clusters of a given cell type (patches of tumor cells, immune aggregates, stromal clusters), concave hulls draw boundaries around each patch, and the analysis then measures how other cell types sit relative to those boundaries: inside, on the border, or nearby.
spacec.patchP3: That picks up tissue-level organization the cell-level view misses. A tumor microenvironment isn't just a set of pairwise interactions; it's the arrangement of which populations sit next to which.
spacec.annDataP: AnnData is the standard data structure in single-cell biology, so building on it lets SPACEc work directly with scanpy. Researchers don't learn a new format, and they can move between the two. The .obs, .var, and .obsm fields map cleanly onto cell metadata, marker metadata, and spatial coordinates.
spacec.cellposeP: Different imaging modalities call for different segmentation. Cellpose does well on cytoplasm with limited training data; DeepCell (Mesmer) is better for nuclear segmentation in whole-cell multiplex images. Supporting both lets researchers pick the right tool without leaving the library.
spacec.gpuP: These datasets get large: tens of thousands of cells per sample, hundreds of samples per study. Leiden clustering that takes about 20 minutes on CPU runs in under 2 on GPU via RAPIDS, which matters when you're iterating.
spacec.scanpyP: Most computational biologists already know sc.tl, sc.pp, and sc.pl. Mirroring it with sp.tl, sp.pp, and sp.pl keeps the learning curve on the science rather than the API, which makes people more likely to actually use the library.
spacec.tradeoffsPython: Python 3.9–3.10 only. TensorFlow, PyTorch, and RAPIDS have version requirements that don't overlap cleanly above 3.10. This is the biggest friction point for adoption, which is why the CPU and GPU Docker images are the recommended install path.
spacec.tradeoffsModules: Monolithic modules. The _general.py files are large and hard to navigate. The scanpy convention keeps the public API consistent (sp.tl.function()) at the cost of file organization. That was a deliberate call: clean API, dense internals.
spacec.tradeoffsDeps: Dependency sprawl. Cellpose, DeepCell, RAPIDS, PyTorch Geometric, GeoPandas, and TensorFlow each pull in transitive dependencies that fight each other, so a clean install outside Docker takes real effort. That's the cost of wrapping the best tool for each step instead of reimplementing them.
spacec.talkTitle: SPACEc: A Streamlined, Interactive Python Workflow
spacec.sn1: Multiplexed imaging stains a single tissue section for 40+ proteins at once — where older methods could resolve only a handful.
spacec.sn2: Following scanpy’s sp.tl/sp.pl convention means anyone who has used scanpy already knows the shape of SPACEc.

terms.metaDescription: Terms of service for klee.page and Dispatch.
terms.heading: Terms of Service
terms.agreementP: By accessing klee.page or using Dispatch, you agree to these terms. If you don't agree, please don't use our services.
terms.serviceP: Dispatch is a platform for creating and sending beautiful personal letters to friends and family. The service includes a mobile app (iOS) and a web reader for recipients.
terms.accountLi1: You must be at least 13 years old (16 in the EU) to create a Dispatch account.
terms.accountLi2: You are responsible for maintaining the security of your account credentials.
terms.accountLi3: You must provide accurate information when creating your account.
terms.accountLi4: You can delete your account at any time from Settings in the app.
terms.accountLi5: One person, one account. Don't create multiple accounts.
terms.contentP1: You own everything you create on Dispatch — your letters, photos, and media belong to you. We don't claim any intellectual property rights over your content.
terms.contentP2: By sending a letter through Dispatch, you grant us a limited, non-exclusive license to store, process, and deliver that content to your intended recipients. We don't use your content for any other purpose.
terms.contentP3: You are responsible for the content of your letters. Don't send anything illegal, harmful, or that violates someone else's rights.
terms.acceptableIntro: You agree not to use Dispatch to:
terms.acceptableLi1: Send unsolicited messages, spam, or bulk communications
terms.acceptableLi2: Harass, threaten, defame, or abuse other people
terms.acceptableLi3: Share content that is illegal, obscene, or violates others' intellectual property rights
terms.acceptableLi4: Attempt to access other users' accounts, letters, or data
terms.acceptableLi5: Reverse engineer, decompile, or attempt to extract our source code
terms.acceptableLi6: Use automated systems to access the service (bots, scrapers, etc.)
terms.acceptableLi7: Interfere with or disrupt the service or its infrastructure
terms.acceptableP: We may suspend or terminate accounts that violate these terms.
terms.aiP: Dispatch offers optional AI-powered writing assistance (polishing, translation, voice-to-letter). These features are provided as-is. AI suggestions are just that — suggestions. You are responsible for reviewing and approving the final content of every letter you send. AI outputs may not always be accurate, appropriate, or complete.
terms.ipP: The Dispatch name, logo, design, and underlying technology are owned by Kevin Lee. You may not use our trademarks or branding without written permission. The open-source components we use retain their respective licenses.
terms.availabilityP1: We strive to keep Dispatch available and reliable, but we can't guarantee 100% uptime. We may perform maintenance, updates, or modifications that temporarily affect availability. We'll provide reasonable notice when possible.
terms.availabilityP2: We reserve the right to modify, suspend, or discontinue any part of the service with reasonable notice. If we discontinue the service entirely, we'll give you at least 30 days to export your data.
terms.paymentP: Dispatch is currently free. If we introduce paid features in the future, we'll notify you in advance. We will never retroactively charge for features that were free when you used them.
terms.liabilityIntro: Dispatch is provided "as is" without warranties of any kind, express or implied. To the maximum extent permitted by law:
terms.liabilityLi1: We are not liable for any indirect, incidental, special, consequential, or punitive damages
terms.liabilityLi2: Our total liability shall not exceed $100 USD or the amount you paid us in the 12 months preceding the claim, whichever is greater
terms.liabilityLi3: We are not responsible for content created or sent by users
terms.indemnificationP: You agree to indemnify and hold harmless Kevin Lee and Dispatch from any claims, damages, or expenses arising from your use of the service or violation of these terms.
terms.governingP: These terms are governed by the laws of the State of California, United States. Any disputes shall be resolved in the courts of Los Angeles County, California.
terms.changesP: We may update these terms from time to time. We'll notify you of material changes via email or in-app notification at least 14 days before they take effect. Continued use after changes constitutes acceptance of the new terms.
terms.severabilityP: If any provision of these terms is found to be unenforceable, the remaining provisions will continue in full force and effect.

privacy.metaDescription: Privacy policy for klee.page and Dispatch.
privacy.heading: Privacy Policy
privacy.overviewP: This privacy policy covers klee.page (personal website) and Dispatch (letter-sending app). Your privacy matters — here's exactly what we do and don't do with your data.
privacy.websiteP: klee.page is a static personal website. It does not collect personal data, use cookies, or track individual visitors. Anonymous analytics are collected via Vercel Web Analytics to understand aggregate traffic patterns (page views, referrers). No personally identifiable information is stored.
privacy.dispatchIntro: Dispatch is a platform for creating and sending personal letters. When you use Dispatch, we collect:
privacy.dispatchLi1: Account information: Name, email, profile photo. Used to identify you and deliver letters.
privacy.dispatchLi2: Letter content: Text, photos, media in your letters. Stored encrypted. Only accessible to you and your recipients.
privacy.dispatchLi3: Device tokens: Push notification tokens. Used solely to notify you when you receive a letter.
privacy.dispatchLi4: Photo metadata: When you use the photo compose feature, EXIF data (GPS coordinates, date) is read locally on your device to auto-tag locations. This data is not sent to our servers unless you choose to include it in your letter.
privacy.dontLi2: We don't sell your data to anyone, ever.
privacy.dontLi4: We don't share your information with third parties for marketing.
privacy.dontLi5: We don't use your content to train AI models.
privacy.dontLi6: We don't track you across websites.
privacy.aiP: When you use AI writing assistance (polish, translate, voice-to-letter), your text is processed by our AI provider to generate suggestions. This content is processed in real-time and is not stored by the AI provider. AI features are always optional — you can write without them.
privacy.storageP: Data is stored on Supabase (hosted on AWS in the US). All data is encrypted at rest and in transit. Database backups are encrypted and retained for 30 days. We use row-level security (RLS) policies to ensure users can only access their own data.
privacy.thirdPartyLi3: Apple: Sign in with Apple, push notifications (APNs)
privacy.thirdPartyLi4: AI providers: Text processing for writing assistance (no data retention)
privacy.rightsLi1: Access: View all your data within the app at any time.
privacy.rightsLi2: Export: Download all your letters and profile data.
privacy.rightsLi3: Correction: Update your profile information in Settings.
privacy.rightsLi4: Deletion: Delete your account and all associated data permanently from Settings > Delete Account. This cannot be undone.
privacy.rightsLi5: Portability: Request a machine-readable export of your data.
privacy.rightsP1: For EU/EEA users: You have additional rights under GDPR including the right to object to processing and the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority.
privacy.rightsP2: For California users: Under CCPA, you have the right to know what personal information we collect, request deletion, and opt out of the sale of personal information (we don't sell your data).
privacy.childrenP: Our services are not intended for users under 13 years of age (16 in the EU). We do not knowingly collect personal information from children. If we learn we have collected data from a child, we will delete it promptly.
privacy.retentionP: We retain your data as long as your account is active. When you delete your account, all personal data is permanently deleted within 30 days. Anonymized, aggregate analytics data may be retained indefinitely.
privacy.changesP: We may update this policy from time to time. Significant changes will be communicated via email or in-app notification. The "Last updated" date at the top reflects the most recent revision.

ai.heading: Ask Kevin
ai.subtitle: An AI that answers as me — engineering, projects, photography, flying. Ask it anything.
ai.disclaimer: AI answers in my voice, from what's on this site. It can be wrong.
ai.errorRate: You're sending messages too fast. Please wait a minute before trying again.
ai.errorNetwork: Sorry, I couldn't respond right now. Please try again.
chips: What are you building at Paramount?
How do you ship 10 side projects?
Why do you still shoot film?
What did the Army teach you about engineering?
